_comment: Realistic mock newsletter (RSS) items. Served by NewsletterSource when feedparser is missing or every live feed blocks. Dates are RFC-822 (real RSS shape); summaries include HTML the adapter strips. All feeds, links, titles and authors are FICTIONAL (reserved example.org hosts) so no fabricated content is ever attributed to a real publication or resolvable URL.

items.title: On-device LLMs go mainstream, Apple ships a 3B local model, and the browser-agent wars heat up
items.summary: Apple's new on-device 3B model runs inference fully offline on the latest silicon, and early benchmarks put it within striking distance of last year's cloud models. Meanwhile every major browser now ships an agent mode — and developers are already complaining about the lack of a shared automation standard.
items.author: Daily Byte

items.title: Vertical AI agents are quietly eating the long tail of SaaS
items.summary: A wave of $20/mo AI agents for niche workflows — dental billing, HOA management, indie-film budgeting — are undercutting incumbents who never bothered to serve those segments. The wedge is always the same: a painful form nobody wanted to build software for.
items.author: Bea Tovey

items.title: The rise of the solo-founder stack: shipping a real product with one engineer
items.summary: A deep dive into how a single engineer now ships production SaaS: managed Postgres, an agent-assisted codebase, and a support inbox triaged by an LLM. The bottleneck has moved from building to distribution and trust.
items.author: Gergo Varga

items.title: Frontier Notes 402: Small models win, synthetic data plateaus, and the compute gap narrows
items.summary: This week: a 7B open model matches last year's frontier on reasoning benchmarks after RL fine-tuning, evidence that synthetic-data returns are flattening, and why the gap between well-funded labs and scrappy teams is narrower than the headlines suggest.
items.author: Casey Marlow

items.title: Building local-first agents you can actually trust
items.summary: I spent the weekend wiring up a fully local agent with tool access sandboxed to a single project directory. The interesting part isn't the model — it's the permission surface. Here's the pattern I landed on and why audit logs matter more than guardrails.
items.author: Sam Wilton

items.title: The unbundling of the CRM: where AI-native GTM tools attack next
items.summary: Salesforce's suite is being picked apart feature by feature by AI-native point tools that do one job — enrichment, sequencing, forecasting — dramatically better. We map the eight wedges we think are most defensible and where the data moat actually lives.
items.author: Beacon Ventures

items.title: Pricing AI products: why usage-based is winning and seats are dying
items.summary: Interviews with 30 AI PMs reveal a clear shift: per-seat pricing breaks the moment an agent does the work of five people. The teams growing fastest have moved to outcome- or usage-based pricing — and the ones stuck on seats are quietly capping their own revenue.
items.author: Danny Rakow

items.title: Voice interfaces finally cross the latency threshold; new sub-200ms speech stacks ship
items.summary: Two open speech-to-speech stacks dropped this week with sub-200ms round-trip latency, low enough that voice agents stop feeling like walkie-talkies. Expect a rush of voice-first products in support, driving, and accessibility over the next two quarters.
items.author: Daily Byte

items.title: "RAG is dead" is the take of the week — here's what's actually replacing it
items.summary: With million-token context windows now cheap, teams are ripping out brittle retrieval pipelines and just loading the whole corpus. But the "just stuff the context" crowd is discovering new failure modes around cost, latency, and needle-in-haystack recall.
items.author: Bea Tovey

items.title: Frontier Notes 401: Robotics foundation models leave the lab and the sim-to-real gap shrinks
items.summary: A shared robotics foundation model fine-tuned on a few hundred real demos now generalizes across grippers and tasks. The sim-to-real gap that stalled warehouse automation for a decade is finally closing — watch for a hardware startup surge.
items.author: Casey Marlow
